title: Benchmarking Large Language Models for Personalized Guidance in AI-Enhanced Learning
abstract: While Large Language Models (LLMs) are increasingly envisioned as intelligent assistants for personalized learning, systematic head-to-head evaluations in authentic learning scenarios remain scarce. This study presents an empirical comparison of three state-of-the-art LLMs on a tutoring task simulating a realistic learning setting. Using a dataset containing a student's responses to ten mixed-format questions with correctness labels, each model was asked to (i) analyze the quiz to identify underlying knowledge components, (ii) infer the student's mastery profile, and (iii) generate targeted guidance for improvement. To mitigate subjectivity and evaluator bias, Gemini was employed as a virtual judge to perform pairwise comparisons across multiple dimensions: accuracy, clarity, actionability, and appropriateness. Results analyzed via the Bradley-Terry model reveal that GPT-4o is generally preferred, producing feedback that is more informative and better structured than its counterparts, whereas DeepSeek-V3 and GLM-4.5 demonstrate intermittent strengths but lower consistency. These findings highlight the feasibility of deploying LLMs as advanced teaching assistants for individualized support and provide methodological insights for subsequent empirical research on LLM-driven personalized learning.
extracted_text:
Benchmarking Large Language Models for Personalized 
Guidance in AI-Enhanced Learning 
Bo Yuan1 and Jiazi Hu2 
1 The University of Queensland, QLD 4072, Australia 
2 AI Consultancy, Kunming, P.R. China 
boyuan@ieee.org, jiazi841231@163.com 
Abstract. While Large Language Models (LLMs) are increasingly envisioned as 
intelligent assistants for personalized learning, systematic head-to-head evalua-
tions in authentic learning scenarios remain scarce. This study presents an empir-
ical comparison of three state-of-the-art LLMs on a tutoring task simulating a 
realistic learning setting. Using a dataset containing a student’s responses to ten 
mixed-format questions with correctness labels, each model was asked to (i) an-
alyze the quiz to identify underlying knowledge components, (ii) infer the stu-
dent’s mastery profile, and (iii) generate targeted guidance for improvement. To 
mitigate subjectivity and evaluator bias, Gemini was employed as a virtual judge 
to perform pairwise comparisons across multiple dimensions: accuracy, clarity, 
actionability, and appropriateness. Results analyzed via the Bradley–Terry model 
reveal that GPT-4o is generally preferred, producing feedback that is more in-
formative and better structured than its counterparts, whereas DeepSeek-V3 and 
GLM-4.5 demonstrate intermittent strengths but lower consistency. These find-
ings highlight the feasibility of deploying LLMs as advanced teaching assistants 
for individualized support and provide methodological insights for subsequent 
empirical research on LLM-driven personalized learning.  
Keywords: Large Language Models, Personalized Learning, AI in Education, 
Intelligent Tutoring, Empirical Evaluation. 
1 
Introduction 
Recently, Large Language Models (LLMs) such as GPT-4o [1], DeepSeek-V3 [2], and 
GLM-4.5 [3] have demonstrated remarkable progress in natural language understand-
ing and generation. Unlike earlier discriminative models that focused on classification 
or prediction within narrow domains, LLMs operate as generative models trained on 
massive corpora, capable of producing contextually coherent text, explanations, and 
even problem-solving strategies [4]. This transition from discriminative to generative 
paradigms marks a profound shift in AI, enabling systems not only to recognize patterns 
but also to synthesize novel multimodal outputs and engage in open-ended interaction. 
These advances have expanded the scope of AI applications and sparked new opportu-
nities in education, where dynamic, adaptive, and human-like feedback has long been 
desired but difficult to achieve [5].  


2  
B. Yuan and J. Hu 
Personalized learning is a prominent application of AI in education, aiming to tailor 
instruction, feedback, and pacing to individual learners’ needs. The lack of personali-
zation has long been recognized as a key factor that reduces learning efficiency, hinders 
conceptual understanding, and diminishes students’ motivation and interest [6, 7]. De-
spite the growing enthusiasm, systematic evidence regarding the effectiveness of LLMs 
in education remains scarce. Existing studies often report the use of a single model 
within a specific, non-standardized learning context [8, 9], making it difficult for other 
researchers to reproduce the experiments or perform fair comparisons. Such case-spe-
cific and opaque practices may hinder cumulative progress, as each study remains iso-
lated rather than contributing to a shared and replicable evidence base. 
Moreover, most existing studies have relied on student test outcomes or expert rat-
ings as the primary criteria for evaluating effectiveness [10, 11]. While informative, 
these measures are inherently limited: student performance is affected by many uncon-
trolled factors beyond the tutoring intervention, and expert judgments are prone to sub-
jectivity and bias [12]. As a result, conclusions drawn from such assessments often lack 
robustness and fail to capture the full spectrum of LLM performance across key peda-
gogical dimensions such as clarity, diagnostic accuracy, and actionability. 
To address this gap, this study focuses on a realistic tutoring scenario. Fig. 1 presents 
an experimental pipeline in which a student’s quiz responses, along with correctness 
labels, are provided to different LLMs. Each model is tasked with identifying the un-
derlying knowledge components, estimating the student’s mastery, and producing per-
sonalized guidance for improvement. For evaluation, Gemini 2.5 [13] serves as an in-
dependent external examiner [14, 15]. This approach mitigates subjectivity by ensuring 
that all judgments are made by a consistent AI model, rather than by human raters who 
may apply divergent standards. Moreover, dependence on human experts is not only 
costly but also impractical, as manual assessments are difficult to scale or customize. 
By contrast, an LLM-powered virtual examiner offers high configurability, allowing 
researchers to tailor evaluation procedure and criteria as needed. These features make 
the evaluation both flexible and scalable, enabling systematic analysis of the strengths 
and weaknesses of different LLMs in realistic learning scenarios.  
Our results suggest that the widely cited leaderboard rankings of LLMs, often pur-
sued within a “benchmark-chasing” culture, may overstate their capabilities, as such 
metrics rarely translate into predictable performance in educational contexts [16, 17]. 
Moreover, this study demonstrates that the empirical evaluation of LLMs can be effec-
tively grounded in realistic learning scenarios to not only mirror authentic pedagogical 
practices but also support multidimensional analyses of LLM capabilities. 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
Fig. 1. Research scenario of post-class tutoring with LLMs. The framework illustrates how learn-
ing data are processed by LLM assistants to generate personalized guidance. 
Learning Data 
LLM Assistants
Personalized 
Guidance 
Quiz 
Responses 
Correctness 
GPT 
GLM 
DeepSeek 
Knowledge Analysis 
Strengths & Weaknesses 
Actionable Suggestions 


 
LLMs for Personalized Guidance 
3 
2 
Background 
2.1 
Personalized Learning 
Personalized learning refers to an instructional approach in which teaching strategies, 
learning resources, and pacing are adapted to the individual needs, abilities, and inter-
ests of each student. Unlike traditional “one-size-fits-all” instruction, personalized 
learning emphasizes differentiation and adaptability, aiming to optimize the learning 
process for every learner. The value of personalized learning has been widely recog-
nized in both educational theory and practice [18]. At its core, personalization enhances 
learning effectiveness by aligning instruction with students’ existing knowledge and 
skills rather than constraining them to the average pace of a classroom. It also promotes 
motivation and engagement, as students receive feedback and tasks that correspond to 
their goals, levels, and preferred modes of learning.  
Historically, individualized tutoring was reserved to privileged learners with access 
to personal mentors. With the expansion of mass education, such individualization be-
came impractical, giving way to standardized curricula and uniform assessments. Sub-
sequent advances in educational technologies, from computer-assisted instruction [19] 
to intelligent tutoring systems [20], sought to reintroduce personalization at scale, 
though often limited by rigid rule-based designs and high development costs. Today, 
the advent of data-driven analytics and AI technologies provides a new opportunity to 
realize personalization in ways that are more flexible, adaptive, and scalable. 
The importance of personalized learning also lies in its capacity to cultivate essential 
student competencies. By receiving feedback and guidance that directly target their 
strengths and weaknesses, learners can develop deeper conceptual understanding and 
stronger problem-solving abilities. Personalization further supports the growth of self-
regulated and lifelong learning skills, as students are encouraged to reflect on their pro-
gress and take ownership of their learning trajectories. 
2.2 
Challenges and the Promise of LLMs 
Although the value of personalized learning has long been acknowledged, achieving it 
within mass education systems remains a persistent challenge. In traditional classroom 
settings, teachers must divide their limited time and attention among many students, 
making it nearly impossible to provide individualized guidance for each learner. More-
over, assessments are often reduced to correctness scores or grades, which provide little 
insight into the underlying misconceptions or partial understanding that shape perfor-
mance. Feedback tends to be generic and uniform, with students receiving the same 
instructional responses regardless of their unique needs. Such limitations may reduce 
learning efficiency, hinder the development of higher-order skills, and ultimately di-
minish student motivation and interest [21, 22]. 
While large-scale education struggles to reconcile efficiency with personalization, 
LLMs introduce a fundamentally new possibility [23, 24], being capable of producing 
dynamic, context-aware, and individualized feedback that moves far beyond static rules 
or pre-programmed scripts. Unlike earlier systems, they can analyze student responses, 


4  
B. Yuan and J. Hu 
diagnose potential misconceptions, and generate tailored guidance in real time [25]. 
This capacity positions LLMs as powerful tools for scalable personalized learning, of-
fering individualized support to learners across diverse education environments. These 
opportunities, however, underscore the need for rigorous and transparent evaluation 
frameworks that can withstand empirical scrutiny and ensure the trustworthiness and 
reproducibility of the findings. While LLMs hold the promise of bridging the long-
standing gap between scale and personalization, key questions remain regarding their 
reliability, clarity, and pedagogical effectiveness in realistic learning contexts.  
3 
Experimental Design 
3.1 
Quiz Data and Student Responses 
To simulate a realistic post-class tutoring scenario, we constructed a dataset of quiz 
items drawn from an undergraduate data structures course. The dataset spans a broad 
range of fundamental knowledge points, including arrays, linked lists, stacks and 
queues, trees, graphs, and algorithmic complexity. Items consist of both true/false and 
short-answer questions, intentionally designed to be concise and unambiguous so that 
LLMs can more easily identify the underlying knowledge points and researchers can 
carry out interpretable analyses.  
Each quiz item was paired with a student response and a correctness label. This setup 
enabled the LLMs to analyze the underlying knowledge points and infer the student’s 
degree of mastery [26]. Notably, the models were not explicitly provided with the 
knowledge points associated with each quiz item; instead, they were required to extract 
and articulate the relevant concepts themselves. This additional step served as an ex-
tended test of diagnostic capability, assessing whether the models could map student 
performance onto established curricular knowledge structures.  
All quiz items, responses, and correctness labels were stored in a standardized JSON 
format. This structural representation ensures that identical inputs can be consistently 
processed by different LLMs, thereby minimizing ambiguity and facilitating reproduc-
ibility. An illustrative example is shown below: 
{ 
    "id": 1, 
    "question": "Explain the difference between a stack and a queue.", 
    "student_answer": "A stack is FIFO and a queue is LIFO.", 
    "correct": false 
 }  
3.2 
Prompt Design for Personalized Guidance 
To ensure fairness and comparability across models, we designed a unified prompt for 
all tested LLMs. The prompt explicitly instructed each model to assume the role of an 
intelligent tutoring assistant specialized in data structures and to generate personalized 
learning guidance based on the quiz dataset. By fixing the prompt content and structure, 


 
LLMs for Personalized Guidance 
5 
we ensured that all LLMs were evaluated under identical conditions, minimizing the 
bias introduced by prompt engineering and allowing differences in output quality to be 
attributed primarily to the models themselves. Specifically, the prompt required the 
LLMs to perform three tasks: i) identify relevant knowledge components for each quiz 
item (e.g., recursion, linked list traversal, binary search tree insertion); ii) infer mastery 
levels by analyzing student responses, categorizing each knowledge point as mastered, 
partially understood, or not yet grasped; and iii) generate personalized feedback, in-
cluding strengths and weaknesses for each concept, likely misconceptions or reasoning 
errors, specific actionable learning suggestions, and recommended resources or practice 
problems. The full prompt is presented as follows: 
You are an intelligent tutoring assistant specialized in data structures. 
You will be given 10 data structure questions, each accompanied by a student’s answer 
and whether the answer is correct or incorrect. 
Your task is to: 
1. Identify the key knowledge points involved in each question (e.g., recursion, linked 
list traversal, binary search tree insertion). 
2. Analyze the student's performance across these questions to infer which knowledge 
areas are: 
- mastered, 
- partially understood, 
- not yet grasped. 
3. Provide a detailed and personalized learning analysis, including: 
- strengths and weaknesses for each identified knowledge point, 
- likely misconceptions or reasoning errors the student may have, 
- specific, actionable learning or review strategies tailored to the student's level, 
- recommended practice problems or resources, if applicable. 
Format your output into clear sections with appropriate subheadings and bullet points. 
Use precise, instructional language suitable for a university-level student. 
3.3 
Prompt Design for Evaluation 
To minimize subjectivity and ensure consistency in evaluation, we employed Gemini 
as an independent external examiner. Unlike the models under test, Gemini was tasked 
exclusively with conducting pairwise evaluations of model outputs. For each compari-
son, it received the same quiz data file along with two versions of personalized guidance 
generated by different LLMs. Its role was to determine which model produced superior 
feedback based on predefined criteria.  
A unified evaluator prompt was carefully designed and applied across all compari-
sons. It explicitly instructed Gemini to act as a strict and consistent evaluator of per-
sonalized learning feedback, focusing on relative quality across five pedagogically 
meaningful dimensions. By relying on relative judgments instead of absolute scoring, 


6  
B. Yuan and J. Hu 
the procedure mitigated evaluator bias and enhanced comparability across models. In 
contrast, absolute scoring often suffers from low discriminative power, as many outputs 
may cluster around similar values, making it difficult to distinguish subtle but important 
differences between models.  
Furthermore, Gemini was instructed to ignore superficial aspects such as formatting 
or sentence length and to concentrate instead on semantic accuracy, logical coherence, 
and pedagogical depth. Each evaluation concluded with a single categorical outcome: 
+1 (Model A preferred), −1 (Model B preferred), or 0 (tie). By enforcing this strict 
evaluation protocol, the procedure promoted balanced utilization of the full decision 
range and ensured that even subtle weaknesses were appropriately penalized whenever 
the alternative output exhibited a discernible level of superiority. The complete evalu-
ator prompt used in the experiments is presented below: 
You are a strict and consistent evaluator of personalized learning feedback. 
## Task 
You will be given: 
1. A question set file containing: 
- 10 data structure questions, 
- a student's answers, 
- whether each answer is correct or incorrect. 
2. Model A's personalized guidance output for this student. 
3. Model B's personalized guidance output for this student. 
Your task: 
1. Compare the output from Model A with the output from Model B. 
2. Evaluate the contents relative to each other across the following five dimensions: 
- accuracy of knowledge diagnosis, 
- specificity and actionability of feedback, 
- identification of misconceptions, 
- instructional clarity, 
- appropriateness to the student’s current level. 
3. Use relative judgment, not absolute scores. 
4. Focus your comparison on semantic meaning, argument structure, logical coher-
ence, evidence strength, and depth of insight, not on superficial details like sentence 
length, formatting, or stylistic phrasing. 
5. After comparing all five dimensions, decide the overall winner: 
-  +1 → Model A is better overall for this pair. 
-  -1 → Model B is better overall for this pair. 
-  0 → They are equally good (or differences are negligible). 


 
LLMs for Personalized Guidance 
7 
## Important Rules 
1. Always use the full range of possible decisions. 
2. If neither output is very good, still pick the better one unless they are truly equal. 
3. Focus on differences, not similarities. 
4. Be strict: small weaknesses should matter if the other output does better. 
5. Do not output explanations or reasoning — only your decision. 
## Output Format 
Name of Model A vs. Name of Model B: +1/0/-1 
4 
Experiments 
4.1 
Workflow Overview 
The workflow (Fig. 2) was designed to simulate an authentic post-class tutoring sce-
nario while enabling a systematic evaluation of multiple LLMs. It comprised four main 
stages: input preparation, personalized guidance generation, external evaluation, and 
analysis. A standardized quiz dataset was first constructed, including data structure 
questions, student responses, and correctness labels. GPT-4o, DeepSeek-V3, and 
GLM-4.5 were then prompted with identical quiz data and instructions (Section 3.2). 
For each run, the models produced personalized guidance outputs that included 
knowledge point extraction, mastery estimation, and targeted learning suggestions. To 
account for output variability, multiple runs were conducted for each model. 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
Fig. 2. Experimental workflow for evaluating LLMs in generating personalized guidance. Quiz 
data are processed by multiple models to generate personalized guidance, which is then assessed 
by an external evaluator to support both quantitative and qualitative analyses. 
Next, model outputs were evaluated by Gemini 2.5, which served as an independent 
external judge performing pairwise comparisons. For each comparison, the evaluator 
received three inputs: the quiz data, Model A’s guidance, and Model B’s guidance. 
Based on this information, it produced relative judgments (+1, 0, −1) across five peda-
gogical criteria: accuracy of knowledge diagnosis, specificity and actionability of feed-
back, identification of misconceptions, instructional clarity, and appropriateness to the 
student’s level. To ensure both statistical rigor and interpretive depth in assessing model

8  
B. Yuan and J. Hu 
performance, a complementary qualitative analysis can be conducted. Such analysis 
may involve examining representative case studies to uncover stylistic nuances, rea-
soning patterns, and qualitative distinctions among models. 
4.2 
Quantitative Analysis 
Pairwise Win–Tie–Loss Results. In each run, all three models generated outputs for 
the same quiz data, after which three pairwise comparisons (GPT-4o vs. DeepSeek-V3, 
GPT-4o vs. GLM-4.5, and DeepSeek-V3 vs. GLM-4.5) were conducted. This proce-
dure was repeated across ten runs, yielding a total of 30 pairwise evaluations. For each 
matchup, Gemini determined which model provided superior feedback, or whether the 
two outputs were of comparable quality. Furthermore, spot checks with repeated runs 
confirmed that the judgments of Gemini were highly consistent across trials, providing 
confidence that its assessments were both stable and reproducible under the chosen 
setup. The outcomes are summarized in Table 1, reported as win–tie–loss counts from 
the perspective of the row model. 
Table 1. Pairwise win–tie–loss results among the three LLMs across 10 runs. 
 
GPT-4o 
DeepSeek-V3 
GLM-4.5 
GPT-4o 
─ 
5–2–3 
9–1–0 
DeepSeek-V3 
3–2–5 
─ 
7–2–1 
GLM-4.5 
0–1–9 
1–2–7 
─ 
Inspection of the win–tie–loss matrix clearly reveals three consistent patterns. First, 
GPT-4o held a decisive advantage over GLM-4.5 and also surpassed DeepSeek-V3 
with a modest yet positive margin (five wins vs. three losses). Second, DeepSeek-V3 
consistently outperformed GLM-4.5, recording seven wins against only one loss. Third, 
GLM-4.5 underperformed relative to both competitors, with no wins against GPT-4o 
and only a single win against DeepSeek-V3. Overall, these outcomes indicate a distinct 
performance hierarchy: GPT-4o > DeepSeek-V3 > GLM-4.5. 
Bradley–Terry Model Analysis. While the pairwise win–tie–loss counts provide di-
rect evidence of comparative performance, they do not readily yield a unified ranking 
of model strengths. To integrate results across all matchups, we employed the Bradley–
Terry (BT) model [27], a probabilistic framework widely used for paired comparison 
data. In this model, each system is assigned a latent strength parameter s, with the prob-
ability of model i being judged superior to model j defined as: 
𝑃ሺ𝑖≻𝑗ሻൌ
௘ೞ೔
௘ೞ೔ା௘ೞೕ    
(1) 
Based on results of the 30 pairwise comparisons, the estimated BT strengths are 
summarized in Table 2 and visualized in Fig. 3 using a forest plot [28]. The strength 
values were mean-centered for interpretability: positive values indicate above-average 


 
LLMs for Personalized Guidance 
9 
performance, while negative values indicate below-average performance. It is evident 
that GPT-4o (BT = 0.95, 95% CI [0.19, 1.72]) achieved the highest strength, with its 
confidence interval lying entirely above zero, indicating statistically significant superi-
ority. DeepSeek-V3 (BT = 0.36, 95% CI [-0.33, 1.06]) occupied an intermediate posi-
tion. Its interval crossed zero, suggesting that although DeepSeek-V3 often produced 
competitive outputs, its advantage was not statistically robust. In contrast, GLM-4.5 
(BT = -1.32, 95% CI [-2.21, -0.43]) was consistently weaker, with a negative strength 
estimate and a confidence interval fully below zero.  
Overall, the BT analysis not only corroborates the win–tie–loss matrix but also yields 
a statistically grounded ranking of model performance. It shows that GPT-4o was sig-
nificantly superior in delivering personalized tutoring feedback, whereas DeepSeek-V3 
exhibited moderate competitiveness, and GLM-4.5 consistently lagged behind.  
Table 2. Bradley-Terry strength estimates with 95% confidence intervals for the three LLMs.

Fig. 3. Forest plot of Bradley-Terry strength estimates with 95% confidence intervals (CI) for the 
three LLMs. Red dots represent point estimates, and horizontal lines indicate 95% CIs. 
Beyond the raw win–tie–loss counts, the Bradley–Terry model offers a principled 
method for converting latent strength estimates into predicted win probabilities, which 
are both more interpretable and statistically grounded. As shown in Table 3, GPT-4o 
was predicted to outperform DeepSeek-V3 with a probability of 64%, and GLM-4.5 
with a probability of 91%, underscoring its consistent superiority. DeepSeek-V3, in 
turn, held a clear advantage over GLM-4.5, with a predicted win probability of 84%. It 
is important to note that these predicted probabilities are not equivalent to the empirical 
win ratios observed in the raw data. Instead, the BT-based predictions are derived from 
a unified latent scale that synthesizes information across all pairwise comparisons, 
yielding a consistent and transitive ranking of model performance.

Fig. 4. UMAP visualization of LLM outputs. It illustrates the 2D UMAP projection of 30 texts 
generated by three LLMs. Each point represents one output, and colors denote different models. 
Finally, the distributions of the model outputs were visualized using UMAP [29], 
where sentence embeddings were generated using the all-MiniLM-L6-v2 model in the 
Sentence-Transformers framework [30]. In Fig. 4, the clusters indicate that GPT-4o and 
GLM-4.5 exhibited relatively close semantic representations, whereas DeepSeek-V3 
formed a distinct cluster at a greater distance. Interestingly, this spatial relationship does 
not align with the previously observed performance rankings. This divergence suggests 
that semantic proximity in embedding space does not necessarily correspond to peda-
gogical quality, highlighting the need for evaluation frameworks that extend beyond 
surface-level textual similarity when assessing LLMs in educational contexts. 
5 
Conclusion and Future Work 
This study addressed a critical gap in understanding the role of LLMs in education. To 
demonstrate how they can be systematically evaluated as personalized assistants, we 
developed an experimental framework that simulates a realistic tutoring scenario. 
Given a quiz dataset, the models were asked to assess learners’ knowledge mastery and 
generate personalized learning guidance. Three leading LLMs, GPT-4o, DeepSeek-V3, 
and GLM-4.5, were evaluated, while Gemini 2.5 served as an external evaluator to 
minimize subjectivity. Using pairwise comparisons and Bradley–Terry modeling, the 
quantitative analysis identified consistent differences among models: GPT-4o generally 
achieved the highest pedagogical quality; DeepSeek-V3 showed intermittent competi-
tiveness; and GLM-4.5 exhibited weaker overall performance.  


 
LLMs for Personalized Guidance 
11 
Importantly, the primary goal of this study is not to establish yet another perfor-
mance hierarchy among LLMs, but rather to elucidate their potential in personalized 
education. Collectively, the findings demonstrate that LLMs hold genuine promise as 
AI teaching assistants capable of delivering individualized and pedagogically meaning-
ful support. More critically, the study reveals that the evaluation of such systems must 
move beyond correctness-based judgments and factors such as clarity, diagnostic depth, 
actionability, and communicative tone are essential to capture their true educational 
value. Given the rapid and ongoing evolution of LLMs, whose capabilities and behav-
iors continue to advance at an unprecedented pace, this work underscores the need for 
transparent and reproducible evaluation frameworks that can serve as methodological 
anchors for empirical research on personalized, AI-driven learning. 
For future work, the scope of evaluation can first be broadened by incorporating 
diverse datasets that cover multiple courses, mixed question types, and varying diffi-
culty levels. Second, the evaluation dimensions can be extended to include fairness, 
adaptivity, engagement, and long-term learning impact. Third, cross-cultural and cross-
linguistic investigations may yield valuable insights into how LLMs adapt to different 
educational contexts, learner backgrounds, and instructional languages [31]. Fourth, 
human-in-the-loop validation [32] is essential for integrating direct feedback from stu-
dents and instructors. Finally, an important avenue is to examine the effectiveness of 
LLM-based tutoring for students with diverse learning habits and preferences [33]. 
Such investigations will help determine whether certain feedback styles are universally 
effective or require adaptation to different learner profiles, thereby advancing the per-
sonalization agenda in AI-enhanced education.
